Tweet_Text:
The assertion that President Uhuru Kenyatta "messed up everything the grand coalition government made" by introducing the Competency-Based Curriculum (CBC) as a "foreign decoy" disregards the global trends in education. While the transition to CBC in Kenya has faced challenges, dismissing it as weak or destabilizing overlooks its alignment with international education standards.

Globally, many countries are shifting towards CBC to equip learners with the practical skills, critical thinking, and creativity needed to thrive in the 21st century. Nations like Finland, Canada, New Zealand, and cĺoser home we have Uganda and Rwanda who have embraced CBC principles, focusing on learner-centered approaches and real-world application of knowledge. By adopting CBC, Kenya is not isolating itself but rather positioning its education system to meet international benchmarks.

This shift from the 8-4-4 system that was heavily exam-oriented and failed to cater to the diverse abilities and interests of learners, particularly enhances their competitiveness in the global job market. With CBC's emphasis on competencies such as collaboration, innovation, and adaptability, Kenyan learners are better prepared to seize opportunities abroad. Employers worldwide increasingly value graduates who can demonstrate these skills, making it easier for Kenyan students to be absorbed into international academic institutions and workplaces.

The challenges President Ruto is addressing in education today are not unique to CBC but rather systemic issues that require collaborative solutions. Blaming past leadership distracts from the need to refine and support this reform. Investing in teacher training, resources, and public engagement will ensure that CBC achieves its transformative potential, benefiting both the nation and its graduates on a global scale.

UHAMIAJI RUKWA YAENDELEA NA UTOAJI WA ELIMU KWA UMMA

Na. A/Insp Latius Basigazi - Rukwa

Idara ya Uhamiaji Mkoa wa Rukwa imeendelea na kampeni ya kutoa elimu kwa umma kwa kutembelea Chuo cha Sumbawanga College of Health and Allied Sciences (SUCHAS) kilichopo kata ya Mazwi, Tarafa ya Lwiche, Wilaya ya Sumbawanga.

Elimu hiyo imetolewa na Maafisa na Askari wa Uhamiaji wakiongozwa na Mkaguzi Msaidizi wa Uhamiaji, Latius Basigazi, ikiwa ni utekelezaji wa maagizo ya Kamishna Jenerali wa Uhamiaji (CGI) Dkt. Anna Makakala kuhakikisha kila Mtanzania anajua majukumu ya Idara ya Uhamiaji.

Afisa Uhamiaji Mkoa wa Rukwa (ACI) Ibrahim Luhizo ameagiza elimu hii isambazwe katika shule, vyuo na taasisi mbalimbali ili kuimarisha uelewa wa wananchi kuhusu masuala ya Uhamiaji.

Miongoni mwa mada zilizofundishwa ni hati za kusafiria (pasipoti) na aina zake, vibali vya ukaazi kwa wageni, pamoja na madhara ya kuwahifadhi wahamiaji haramu.

Wananchi na wanafunzi wa chuo hicho walipokea elimu hii kwa mwitikio mzuri na walihimizwa kushirikiana na Idara ya Uhamiaji katika kutoa taarifa za wahamiaji haramu kwa ajili ya usalama wa Taifa.

MAFUNZO MAALUMU YA UHAMIAJI KWA WADAU YAHITIMISHWA

Na CPL DALLU PL

Chuo cha Uhamiaji cha Kikanda (TRITA) kimehitimisha mafunzo ya Essentials of Migration Management in Tanzania (EMMIT) kwa wadau wa uhamiaji, wakiwemo wataalamu wa rasilimali watu (HR), sheria, mahusiano ya umma, maafisa waandikishaji (waajiri), wamiliki wa biashara, na wengine wanaojihusisha na masuala ya kiuhamiaji.

Mafunzo haya ya siku tano yalifunguliwa na kufungwa rasmi na Kamishna Mstaafu Maurice David Kitinusa, ambaye aliwapongeza washiriki kwa kupata ujuzi wa kipekee katika usimamizi wa masuala ya kiuhamiaji.

Mafunzo haya yanawajengea uwezo wadau wetu katika nyanja mbalimbali kama:

✅ Kuelewa sheria, kanuni, na taratibu za uhamiaji
✅ Upatikanaji wa vibali vya kazi na ukaazi, pasipoti, uraia na taratibu zake
✅ Kuimarisha ushirikiano kati ya sekta binafsi na Idara ya Uhamiaji kwa ufanisi zaidi
✅ Kuboresha utendaji wa taasisi kwa kuhakikisha uzingatiaji wa sheria za uhamiaji
✅ Kupanua mtandao wa kitaalamu (networking) kati ya wadau na Idara ya Uhamiaji

Mkuu wa Chuo, Kamishna Msaidizi Hoja Mahiba, amewataka wadau wote kuchangamkia fursa hii kwani ni muhimu kwa utekelezaji wa majukumu yao kwa kuzingatia sheria za nchi.

Mafunzo haya hufanyika mara nne kwa mwaka, na awamu inayofuata inatarajiwa kufanyika Mei 2025.

Washiriki walihitimisha kwa mafanikio na kutunukiwa vyeti vya ushiriki. Hongereni kwa hatua hii muhimu! 🎉👏

#🇹🇿UhamiajiUpdates #🇹🇿UhamiajiHabari #MjueJiraniYako🇹🇿

UFUNGUZI WA MAFUNZO YA EMMIT 2025

Na. CPL. Dallu PL.

Chuo cha Uhamiaji cha Kikanda (TRITA) leo tarehe 10 Machi 2025 kimefungua rasmi mafunzo ya Essentials of Immigration Management in Tanzania (EMMIT) 2025, ambayo yataendelea hadi tarehe 14 Machi 2025. Mafunzo haya yanalenga kuwajengea uwezo wadau wa uhamiaji katika usimamizi wa wageni ndani ya taasisi zao kwa kuzingatia sheria, kanuni, na miongozo ya uhamiaji.

Mgeni rasmi katika hafla hiyo alikuwa Kamishna Mstaafu wa Uhamiaji, David Maulice Kitinusa, ambaye amesisitiza umuhimu wa kuwa na uelewa wa kina wa taratibu za vibali vya ukaazi kwa wageni ili kuepusha changamoto za kisheria na kuimarisha taswira ya Tanzania kimataifa.

Mkuu wa Chuo, Kamishna Msaidizi wa Uhamiaji Hoja Mahiba, ameeleza kuwa mafunzo haya ni fursa muhimu kwa wadau wa uhamiaji ili kuhakikisha usalama wa taifa, maendeleo ya kiuchumi, na uboreshaji wa mazingira ya uwekezaji.

Mafunzo haya yanashirikisha wataalamu kutoka Idara ya Uhamiaji, Idara ya Kazi, na IOM/ACBC (International Organization for Migration / African Capacity Building Centre). Mada zinazojadiliwa ni pamoja na:

✅ Hati za kusafiria
✅ Vibali vya ukaazi kwa wageni
✅ Uraia wa Tanzania
✅ Usimamizi wa mipaka
✅ Sheria za uhamiaji

Washiriki wanatoka katika taasisi mbalimbali za umma na binafsi zinazoshughulika na wageni. Mafunzo yataendelea kwa siku tano yakihusisha mawasilisho ya wataalamu, mijadala ya vikundi, na mazoezi ya vitendo.

Hafla ya ufunguzi imehitimishwa kwa picha za pamoja huku washiriki wakijiandaa kuendelea na vipindi vya siku zijazo.

#UhamiajiUsalamaNaMaendeleo🇹🇿 #MjueJiraniYako🇹🇿

Ujumbe huo ulitembelea Ndaki ya Sayansi za Ardhi na Uhandisi pamoja na Ndaki ya Sayansi Asilia na Hisabati, ambako ujenzi wa majengo ya madarasa na Maabara unaendelea. 

Ujumbe huo umehimiza Menejimenti kuhakikisha wakandarasi wanakamilisha  kazi ndani ya muda uliowekwa na kuheshimu makubaliano ya Mkataba kwa ubora unaostahili.

The new cohort of the UNIV'R program has officially launched—available scholarships for refugees to pursue a Master’s degree in French or English at @sciencespo  Paris. 
 
Eligibility Criteria:
-Refugees individually recognized by UNHCR or a state.
-Currently residing in the country of asylum outside the EU (resettled refugees are not eligible to apply).
-Hold at least a Bachelor’s degree in a relevant field of study.
-Have a B2 or C1 language level in English and/or French.
-Be aged between 19 and 40 years old.

Additional requirements - @sciencespo Paris:
-Academic transcripts.
-Academic recommendation letters.
-A high GPA.

The call will be open until February 23, 2025.

Tweet_Text:
Belief begins where knowledge ends.
You don’t believe what you know you believe what you don’t know. That’s why it’s disturbing when the @KenyaGovernors flag alarm over a planned performance audit by the @OAG_Kenya targeting bursaries, scholarships, and ECDE services. That reaction speaks volumes.

If public funds meant to uplift poor Kenyan children are above scrutiny, what hope do we have?

The Auditor General must move swiftly and boldly. Kenyans deserve transparency on how their taxes are used or misused for the education of the underprivileged.

Corruption is a cancer bleeding our country dry, denying access to education & healthcare and basic dignity. And it’s not just in counties; it’s rampant in CDF, national projects, and development programs.

The red flags are everywhere.

We also call on @Senate_KE, @NAssemblyKE, @GovtOfKenya, to join in demanding accountability and protecting public resources.

When you know, you don’t believe you act.
It’s time to act.

The lectures of opportunity for the Intermediate Command and Staff Course participants continue at Prisons Academy and Training School. This morning, Mr. Eli Muhumuza, Assistant Commissioner General of Prisons (Director Research, Policy, Planning and Project Development), discussed UPS’s strategies for achieving the institutional mandate.

He emphasized infrastructure development, offender rehabilitation, and a business-oriented approach, highlighting mechanized prison farms, irrigation, and industrial workshops for furniture, leather and soap. 

Mr. Muhumuza stressed ICT integration, health improvements, and parole systems. Certified skills training, cottage industries, and public-private partnerships balance rehabilitation and commercial viability, while staff welfare enhances through SACCOs and housing, aligning UPS with Uganda’s development goals.

Tweet_Text:
The Prisons Academy and Training School was a beehive of activity today as Heads of Directorates from Prison Headquarters delivered insightful lectures to Intermediate Command and Staff Course participants who are nearing completion.

Mr. Hillary Bisanga, Assistant Commissioner General of Prisons (Director Human Resource Management), led the first session, focusing on career growth. He highlighted the Uganda Prisons Service's exemplary human resource policies and standards, which ensure robust staff development through structured recruitment for Warders/Wardresses, Cadet Principal Officers, and Cadet Assistant Superintendents of Prisons.

The training includes in-service programs, professional courses at Uganda Management Institute (UMI) and Law Development Centre (LDC), and ideological training at National Leadership Institute (NALI) among others.

Mr. Bisanga emphasized discipline, ambition, mindset change, continuous learning, gender mainstreaming, and retirement planning as key career pillars.

Today, Uganda Prisons Service joined efforts with the Church of Uganda to raise funds for the construction of a boys’ hostel adjacent to Mengo Senior Secondary School. The Do Good Family Run organised by Mothers Union Fraternity of Kampala Diocese, was held under the theme “Imitating God’s Goodness by Doing Good” (Galatians 6:9–10), it brought together Church leaders, Mothers Union, Prisons officers, students, and Christians in a show of unity and purpose.

The Commissioner General of Prisons, Canon Dr. Johnson Byabashaija, was the Chief Runner of the event. The day started at 6:00 a.m. with a warm-up aerobics session, after which Dr. Byabashaija flagged off the 5-kilometre run from Mengo SS around Kampala Diocese, alongside Archbishop Dr. Stephen Kaziimba Mugalu.

Ntungamo District — Thousands of enthusiastic supporters on Thursday thronged Kyamate Primary School grounds as NRM Presidential Candidate Yoweri Kaguta Museveni and First Lady Maama Janet Museveni returned to Ntungamo, drawing one of the largest crowds of the campaign trail so far.

Museveni, who described Ntungamo as a place he “passed through twice with a gun” during the liberation struggles of 1979 and 1985, told supporters that his return in peace symbolized how far the country had come.

“Today I told the people of Isingiro that thrice I passed through Isingiro fighting. Here in Ntungamo, I passed twice with a gun. But now we have come in peace, and I must thank God for that,” he said. “God has used NRM to bring peace to Uganda.”

SCOS Concludes Two-Day Waste Management Training, Lauds Partners for the Support 

Nkumba University’s School of Sciences has commended its partners, including Petroleum Authority of Uganda (PAU), TotalEnergies EP Uganda, E360 Group Ltd, and Enviroserve Ltd, for supporting academic capacity building following a successful two-day Safety, Health, Environment, and Quality (SHEQ) Waste Management training.

Held from March 26th to 27th, the training brought together lecturers under the Tilenga Train-the-Trainer (TTT) program, an initiative designed to equip university instructors with practical, industry-driven competencies to better prepare students for emerging opportunities within Uganda’s oil and gas and environmental management sectors.

Speaking after the training, Ameria Nabukonde, the Head, Natural Resources Management at Nkumba University, thanked partners for supporting capacity building within academia. “We thank our partners, trainers, and academic staff for the commitment demonstrated during this training. This program is critical in strengthening national capacity for SHEQ excellence while improving the quality of instruction we deliver to our students,” Nabukonde said.

With Uganda’s extractives sector expected to expand in the coming years, stakeholders believe that continued collaboration between industry and academia will be critical in ensuring that the country develops the skilled workforce required to support safe and sustainable development.

Tweet_Text:
Danstan Omari is a respected Kenyan advocate, lecturer, and one of the country’s most sought-after lawyers. Born on 6th December 1964 in Nyabite village, Nyamira County, he rose from humble beginnings through sheer determination.
He attended Nyamira Primary School (19/36 marks), Ruiru Secondary (Division 3), and later Kiabonyoru High School (Division 2, alongside Fred Matiang’i). He completed A-levels at Muguga High School and graduated with a Bachelor of Education from Kenyatta University in 1990. Driven by his passion for law, he earned an LLB from the University of Nairobi in 2008, enrolled at the Kenya School of Law, and completed a Master of Laws (LLM) in 2011. He also attended a three-month Juvenile Delinquency programme in Japan. 
Omari began as an untrained teacher in 1986. After university, he faced multiple TSC interdictions but continued teaching and took up roles as a hotel and club manager. In 2004, he enrolled for law while working as a Children’s Officer in Kirinyaga. He was admitted as an advocate on 4th November 2011, joined Musyoki Mogaka & Company Advocates, and later became a law lecturer at Catholic University.Personal LifeHe is married to Pamela, whom he met near Kiabonyoru High School. He fully supported her university education (Bachelor’s and Master’s degrees).Danstan Omari’s journey is a powerful story of resilience, ambition, and unwavering pursuit of his dreams.

Elimu hiyo ilitolewa na Mkaguzi Msaidizi wa Uhamiaji (A/Insp) Isack Nyembwe na (A/Insp) Habiba A. Ally, wakishirikiana na Konstebo wa Uhamiaji Nassor M. Khamiss na Konstebo Ally H. Juma.

Katika mafunzo hayo, wanafunzi walipata uelewa juu ya:
✅ Maana ya Uraia na aina zake
✅ Huduma mbalimbali zitolewazo na Idara ya Uhamiaji Tanzania
✅ Wahamiaji haramu na madhara yao kwa Taifa

Kupitia kampeni ya "Mjue Jirani Yako", iliyoanzishwa na Kamishna Jenerali wa Uhamiaji, Dr. Anna Peter Makakala, Idara ya Uhamiaji Mkoa wa Mara ilihamasisha wanafunzi kuelewa umuhimu wa kushirikiana na Uhamiaji kwa kufuatilia wageni katika mazingira yao na kuripoti taarifa zozote za wasiwasi kwa vyombo vya ulinzi na usalama.

Wanafunzi wa shule hiyo waliishukuru Idara ya Uhamiaji kwa elimu hiyo, wakieleza kuwa hawakuwahi kupata maarifa ya Kiuhamiaji hapo awali. Pia, waliahidi kushirikiana na Uhamiaji kwa kutoa taarifa kwa haraka, ili kudumisha usalama na amani ndani ya Kata ya Mwisenge, Wilaya ya Musoma, Mkoa wa Mara.

UHAMIAJI ARUSHA YATOA ELIMU KATIKA SHULE YA SEKONDARI BARAA.

Idara ya Uhamiaji Mkoa wa Arusha leo tarehe 21 Machi 2025 imetoa elimu kwa wanafunzi wa shule ya sekondari Baraa iliyopo wilayani Arusha Mkoa wa Arusha.

Afisa Uhamiaji kata ya Baraa, Const. Abdulaziz Towo, ameongoza utoaji wa elimu hiyo kwa wanafunzi. Ameeliza masuala mbalimbali ya ki-uhamiaji.

✅️ Dira na dhamira ya Idara ya Uhamiaji.
✅️ Mchakato wa hati za safari, viza pamoja na vibali vya ukaazi.
✅️ Huduma bora kwa wateja na Uhamiaji mtandao.

Idara ya Uhamiaji Tanzania inaamini kwamba elimu ni chombo muhimu katika kuboresha uelewa wa masuala ya uhamiaji. Kwa kupitia mpango huu, wanafunzi watakuwa na maarifa ya kutosha na uwezo wa kushiriki vizuri katika kujenga jamii yenye uelewano na ushirikiano kati ya raia na raia wa kigeni.

Elimu hii inatolewa mashuleni ili kusaidia wanafunzi kuelewa sheria na taratibu za Uhamiaji, haki na wajibu wao, pamoja na mchango wa raia wa kigeni katika maendeleo ya Nchi.

UHAMIAJI MKOA WA ARUSHA YATOA ELIMU YA KI-UHAMIAJI NA KAMPENI ENDELEVU YA "MJUE JIRANI YAKO"

Arusha, 19 Machi 2025 – Idara ya Uhamiaji Mkoa wa Arusha leo imetoa elimu ya Ki-uhamiaji kwa wanafunzi wa Shule ya Sekondari Kimandolu, iliyopo wilaya ya Arumeru, mkoani Arusha.

Afisa Uhamiaji kata ya Kimandolu, Const. Elia Kingu, ameongoza utoaji wa elimu hiyo kwa wanafunzi, akisisitiza umuhimu wa kampeni ya “Mjue Jirani Yako”, ambayo inalenga kuwajengea wananchi uelewa kuhusu usalama wa mipaka na udhibiti wa wahamiaji haramu.

Katika mafunzo hayo, wanafunzi walijifunza kuhusu:

✅ Usimamizi na udhibiti wa mipaka – Uingiaji na utokaji wa watu nchini kwa kufuata sheria.
✅ Huduma za viza na vibali vya ukaazi – Wageni waingiao Tanzania wanapaswa kupata vibali halali kwa mujibu wa sheria.
✅ Doria na misako – Udhibiti wa wahamiaji haramu nchini na madhara yao kwa usalama wa taifa.

Lengo kuu la elimu hii ni kuongeza uelewa wa wanafunzi kuhusu kazi na majukumu ya Idara ya Uhamiaji, pamoja na kuwasaidia kuelewa umuhimu wa sheria za uhamiaji katika jamii.

Afisa Uhamiaji kata amewasihi wanafunzi kuwa wazalendo, wakitambua kuwa wao ni viongozi wa kesho, hivyo wanapaswa kushiriki katika kulinda usalama wa nchi kwa kutoa taarifa pale wanapobaini uwepo wa wageni wasiokuwa na vibali halali.

Updates From Rukiga District: Visit to Bukinda Secondary School by ONC Coordinators.

 Earlier today, Bob Akanyijuka, ONC Coordinator for Rukiga District, and Nabuto Joweria, Muzzukulu for Kigezi Sub-region, visited Bukinda Secondary School (USE), where they met with Deputy Headteacher Twinomujuni Paphra.

 Several challenges facing the school were discussed, alongside the school’s appreciation for the government's support over the years, which has led to improved performance in national exams. 👏

🟢 Key Challenges Facing Bukinda Secondary School:

✅ Lack of a Standard Laboratory: The school urgently needs a fully-equipped lab to boost science education 🧪.

✅ Inadequate Computer Lab: Only 16 computers in makeshift rooms are available, far from meeting the needs of a growing student body 💻.

✅ Classroom Shortage: With increased enrollment, there’s an urgent need for a four-classroom block to accommodate more students 🏫.

✅ Staff Accommodation: Better housing for staff is crucial for their well-being and retention 🏡.

✅ Substandard Library: The library lacks proper facilities, affecting students' access to learning resources 📚.

Despite these challenges, Deputy Headteacher Twinomujuni expressed gratitude to the Government of Uganda and President Kaguta Museveni for the continued support, which has been pivotal in the school’s operations and success. 🇺🇬

🟢 School Statistics:

✅ Total Enrollment: 522 students
✅ Females: 264
✅ Males: 258

Bukinda Secondary School, located in Muhanga Town Council, Rukiga District, continues to provide education to a growing student population. However, addressing these infrastructure challenges is key to ensuring even better performance and a brighter future for its students!.💡🚀

How are government schools performing in your areas? lets us know in the comments section👇

Bridging the Digital Divide: Our Visit to Masaku School for the Physically Disabled

Last Friday, after wrapping up video production work at Mawingu’s HQ in Nairobi, we hit the road to Machakos to witness first-hand the transformative power of internet connectivity in special needs education.

At Masaku School for the Physically Disabled, we spent time with the head teacher, teachers, and students, capturing their perspectives on how access to the internet—through the Mawingu Foundation and Microsoft—has transformed their lives. 

What stood out was how deeply integrated the internet has become in daily learning and school operations. From enhanced lesson delivery to improved learning outcomes, the impact is undeniable. Learners can now access valuable educational resources, conduct research, and engage with interactive digital content, making their studies more engaging and effective.

This visit reinforced a fundamental truth—digital inclusion is not just about connectivity; it’s about empowerment. It’s about ensuring that every learner, regardless of ability, has access to the tools and opportunities they need to learn, grow, and open opportunities. 

At Dotsavvy, we are honoured to document these powerful stories of impact alongside Mawingu and Microsoft, showcasing how technology is reshaping the future of education in Kenya.

Stay tuned as we continue this journey to highlight more incredible stories of digital transformation and accessibility!

ONC UPDATES: Monitoring The Performance Of Government Schools Across Uganda

Earlier today, ONC Coordinator for Rukiga District, Mr. Bob Akanyijuka, visited Rwamucuucu Seed School in Kihorezo Parish, Rwamucuucu Sub-county, Rukiga District, where he engaged in discussions with Deputy Headteacher, Mr. Bwikizo Rodgers. 

During their discussion, several challenges faced by the school were highlighted including the following.

✅The school has been without electricity for three years, despite having computers.

✅The increasing student enrolment exceeds the available classroom spaces.

✅While the government initially planned for O’level, the A’ level section is operating without proper infrastructure.

✅The school  also lacks sufficient land for expansion. 

✅Staff quarters of the school also require renovations.

✅Absenteeism, particularly on market days, remains a problem for the school.

✅High operational costs due to poor road access in this hard-to-reach area.

✅Low staffing levels, with the school lacking teachers for subjects like History, Physical Education, and Agriculture, despite promises from the government.

Currently, the school has an enrolment of 456 students, with additional students expected to join after the upcoming Senior 5 intake.

A Journey to Isiolo: How Internet Access is Transforming Learning at Kampi Ya Juu Integrated School

On Tuesday, we embarked on a challenging yet deeply rewarding journey from Nanyuki to Isiolo to visit Kampi Ya Juu Integrated School, a learning institution that serves both visually impaired and sighted students. The visit was, without a doubt, one of the most emotionally moving experiences of our entire journey.

Nestled off the beaten track, the school required navigating rough terrain to reach, but what we found was nothing short of remarkable. Kampi Ya Juu operates on a hybrid power setup, connected to the national grid while also utilizing solar energy, ensuring uninterrupted learning.

Through the internet access provided by Mawingu in partnership with Microsoft, the school has completely transformed the learning experience for both students and teachers. We saw firsthand how assistive technologies like JAWS and the Orbit Reader are used to download and access digital content, empowering visually impaired learners to engage, study, and interact with educational materials independently.

One of the most interesting facilities was at the resource center, where a teacher downloads learning and curriculum materials from the internet and embosses them into Braille—enabling students to study effectively both in classroom sessions and for revision purposes.

Beyond the classroom, the internet has enhanced the personal lives of students and teachers, eliminating the need for expensive mobile data bundles or long commutes to distant cybercafes. We also learned how AI-powered tools allow visually impaired students to take pictures of plants and other objects, which are then described through audio on their smartphones, making learning more interactive and immersive.

We were also joined by the American Tower Corporation (ATC) Kenya team, who, through their partnership with Mawingu, have deployed infrastructure to support connectivity and digital inclusion in schools as part of their corporate social responsibility (CSR) initiatives.

To wrap up an already incredible day, the students and teachers surprised us with a musical performance, that also featured a song that included Mawingu and Microsoft in the chorus! 🎶

This visit reinforced just how life-changing internet access can be in any part of Kenya. It’s not just about being connected—it’s about empowerment, education, and creating opportunities for every learner, regardless of ability.

At Dotsavvy, we feel so privileged to capture these transformative stories of digital inclusion and accessibility. Stay tuned for more from this inspiring journey!

Tweet_Text:
Joint monitoring and evaluation delegation from Mastercard Foundation and Cyber School Technology Solutions was at Nkumba University to assess the progress and impact of the ongoing digital learning supported through the Cyber School - Mastercard Foundation Uganda eLearning Initiative.

The monitoring exercise, conducted at the university’s main campus, focused on key thematic areas including institutional eLearning capacity strengthening, digital infrastructure, curriculum integration, digital inclusion, monitoring and evaluation, and communication strategies.

Welcoming the delegation, Nkumba University Vice Chancellor, Prof. Jude Lubega commended the partnership for its continued support of the institution’s eLearning agenda. “We are grateful for this collaboration, which has significantly strengthened our eLearning department and positioned the university to better respond to the evolving demands of digital education,” he said.

The Cyber School Team Lead, John Mukiibi, thanked the university for its commitment, describing it as a reliable partner. However, he underscored the need for critical reflection. “While notable strides have been made, it is important that we identify and address bottlenecks that may be hindering the full success of eLearning implementation,” he said.

Discussions during the meeting drew participation from various stakeholders, with Cyber School’s Julius Opio noting that although lecturers had been trained to champion eLearning among peers, the expected multiplier effect was yet to be fully realized. “Lecturers trained as ToTs have done a lot, but the multiple effects have not been realized,” he observed.

On the university’s efforts, the Academic Registrar, Dr. Frank Pio Kiyingi highlighted initiatives undertaken to promote digital learning. “We have put in effort to popularize eLearning among students. We have also developed a course to support this transition,” he said.

Nkumba University is among seven higher institutions of learning in Uganda benefiting from the Cyber School–Mastercard Foundation eLearning Initiative. The program has supported the construction of a state-of-the-art eLearning studio, the provision of assistive digital devices for students with disabilities, and capacity building for lecturers.

The initiative is part of broader efforts to expand access to inclusive, technology-driven education in Uganda, a push that has gained momentum in recent years, particularly following the disruptions caused by the COVID-19 pandemic, which exposed gaps in digital learning preparedness across the education sector.

#I_Owe_You

Tweet_Text:
Today, we launched our School of Law Students' Moot Court in a ceremony presided over by the Principal Judge of Uganda, Hon. Lady Justice Jane Frances Abodo.

In her address , the Principal Judge described the facility as one of the most advanced of its kind in the country.

She commended the University for investing in a facility that meets the required standards for legal training and strengthens the foundation of justice delivery in Uganda. "This kind of investment directly influences the quality of justice delivery,” Justice Abodo said. “What happens in our courts is, to a large extent, shaped by the quality of lawyers trained in institutions such as this. It is a step in the right direction for the future of justice in Uganda.”

She noted that while Uganda continues to produce an increasing number of law graduates, gaps remain in practical training, particularly in courtroom advocacy and case management. She emphasized that moot courts are essential in bridging this gap by equipping students with hands-on litigation skills. “A well-trained advocate assists the court in arriving at a just and fair decision. But when training is inadequate, it reflects in how cases are handled,” she observed. “Moot courts help students refine their professionalism, and ethical conduct before they enter real practice.”

In his remarks, Nkumba University Vice Chancellor, Prof  Jude Lubega, said the establishment of the moot court aligns with ongoing reforms under the competence-based curriculum as guided by the National Council for Higher Education. "Today’s Moot Court launch speaks directly to a broader transformation in higher education, our transition towards a competence-based curriculum,” said Prof. Jude. “The world no longer measures education solely by what one knows, but increasingly by what one can do. In the field of law, this distinction is even more critical.”

He described the facility as “a laboratory of legal practice” where students will develop advocacy skills, confidence, and ethical judgment.

Prof. Lubega further urged the School of Law to embrace digital innovation, including virtual simulations and live-streaming of proceedings, to align with global trends in legal practice. “Court proceedings, legal research, and dispute resolution are increasingly moving into virtual and hybrid spaces. This facility should not remain confined within physical walls,” he said.

The moot court is expected to provide students with a simulated courtroom environment to practise litigation, sharpen research and oratory skills, and gain hands-on experience before entering the legal profession.

Globally, moot courts remain central to legal education, offering exposure to mock trials and appellate arguments. In Uganda, however, such facilities have been limited, particularly within private universities, making Nkumba University’s investment a notable milestone.

SOSS Celebrates Unity in Diversity at Annual Dinner

Nkumba University’s School of Social Sciences (SOSS) held its Annual Dinner on Saturday May 2nd, 2026, at Admas Grand Hotel in Entebbe under the theme, “Unity in Diversity: Celebrating Social Sciences Together.”

The colorful event brought together students, lecturers, and leaders at the School of Social Sciences in a celebration aimed at promoting togetherness, inclusivity, and unity within the institution. 

During the dinner, Ayebare Cristone was sworn in as the new SOSS President, marking a new chapter in student leadership at the school.

Outgoing SOSS President Kivumbi Edward thanked those in attendance and called for continued unity, "Promoting unity in diversity is important. As a school, we come together to celebrate, and we hope to make this an annual unifying activity for the School,” he said.

Speaking at the event, the School Dean, Dr. Anne Abaho emphasized the important role social scientists play in society and encouraged students to uphold values of tolerance and inclusivity. “The fact that we are social means we are expected to be tolerant, inclusive, and willing to guide others. Social scientists are key in making impactful contributions to our society,” she noted.

The first of its kind, the SOSS Annual Dinner will now be held every year and organized by the School of Social Sciences Society leadership.

Today I am not writing about a missing child.
Today I am crying for dead children.

Little girls who woke up for school, folded their uniforms, laughed with friends, called home, dreamed about tomorrow… and never made it out alive.

These photos are not just pictures.

They are somebody’s daughters.
Somebody’s entire world.

Two beautiful little souls reduced to painful memories because of a school inferno that has left this country bleeding.

What do you tell a mother who was waiting for her daughter to come home?

What do you tell a father whose phone will never ring again with “Dad, I miss you”?

The silence in those homes tonight is louder than screams.

Empty beds.

Unopened books.

Shoes that will never be worn again.

This pain is unbearable. Kenya is mourning children. 

Innocent children.

This afternoon I paid a courtesy call on my UoN Med School teacher who’s the founding father of Rheumatology in Kenya and the region - the legendary Prof. George Omondi Oyoo - after speaking earlier and requesting a visit to his clinic.

Interestingly, despite Prof. Oyoo having interacted with members of my DKL team at conferences including Kenya Association of Physicians (KAP) meeting - which I missed this year in Mombasa while attending WCN in Tokyo Japan - and also interacting with our laboratory reports over time, this was actually my very first one-on-one meeting with him since founding Dr. Kalebi Labs (DKL) one and a half years ago.

I made the short trip from DKL to his clinic and, despite his incredibly busy schedule, we had a brief but very rich, insightful and engaging discussion around lab medicine, diagnostics, rheumatology and the rapidly evolving healthcare landscape in Kenya and the region.

I was particularly humbled and encouraged by his kind remarks on the innovativeness, uniqueness and clinician-oriented approach of our lab reporting systems at DKL.

Prof. Oyoo has always been an immensely inspirational physician, academic and mentor whose towering contribution to rheumatology, medical training, research and patient care across Africa continues to impact generations of clinicians. His remarkable academic accomplishments - including earning two PhDs - reflect a lifelong commitment to excellence, scholarship and service.

I now look forward to welcoming Prof. Oyoo to DKL soon as per our discussion in order to showcase our first-of-its-kind ultramodern homegrown lab as we explore further shaping rheumatology lab practice in the New Era of Laboratory Medicine 🙏🏾🩺

Tweet_Text:
The greatest fraud ever committed against Kenyans is making poor people believe stolen public money belongs to tribes and political parties.

When trillions are looted from hospitals, schools, roads, fertilizer subsidies, youth funds and county budgets, there is no Kikuyu money, Luo money, Kalenjin money, Kamba money or Kisii money being stolen. It is Kenyan money. OUR money.

The child sent home for school fees in Kibra suffers the same pain as the farmer in Eldoret, the mama mboga in Kisii, the boda boda rider in Machakos and the fisherman in Homa Bay. Corruption does not discriminate. Hunger has no tribe. Poverty has no party card.

That is why politicians work day and night to divide citizens using tribes, because once Kenyans unite and realize the theft is national, the political class collapses overnight. They know an angry Luo can join hands with an angry Kikuyu, a frustrated Kalenjin and a jobless Luhya and ask the most dangerous question any corrupt regime fears:

“Where did our money go?”

The looters survive by making citizens defend thieves from their own communities instead of defending their own future. They want Kenyans fighting each other while a small cartel empties the treasury in broad daylight.

This country will only change the day Kenyans stop voting emotionally as tribes and start thinking collectively as taxpayers. Because the taxes stolen from a vegetable vendor in Nairobi, a tea farmer in Kericho and a hawker in Mombasa all end up in the same pockets.

Kenya does not have a tribal corruption problem. Kenya has a leadership and accountability problem. Until citizens understand that, politicians will continue getting rich while ordinary people continue burying dreams, businesses and loved ones.

Season's greetings from our Vice Chancellor, Prof. Jude Lubega. 

Dear members of Nkumba University community; our staff, students, parents, guardians, alumni, partners, and friends.

As this year draws to a close, I extend heartfelt season’s greetings to you all.
2025 has been a year of growth, resilience, and renewed purpose for Nkumba University. 

Together, we have expanded strategic partnerships, advanced research and innovation, and deepened our engagement with the communities we serve.

I sincerely thank our dedicated staff for your professionalism and commitment; our students for your discipline, creativity, and ambition; our parents and guardians for your trust; and our partners for walking this journey with us.

This year, we celebrated several major milestones, including our 27th Graduation ceremony, hosting the Guild Leaders Patriotism training, the launch of the Nkumba University Rotary WASH Institute, digital transformation initiatives, and impactful community programs such as the Digitally Enabled Early Childhood Development Program for Primary Schools (DECEDA).

These achievements remind us that Nkumba University is more than a place of learning; it is a community committed to shaping creativity, character, competence, and confidence for service to our society and the world over.

The festive season is upon us, I want to encourage each one of you to take time to rest, reflect, and reconnect with family and loved ones. Let us carry forward the spirit of gratitude, unity, and hope into the new year.

May the season bring you peace, good health, and renewed strength.

Merry Christmas and a Happy New Year 2026.

I owe you.

Prof. Jude T. Lubega 
Vice Chancellor 
Nkumba University

Nkumba University Concludes Two-Day Master’s Research Seminar 

Nkumba University has concluded a two-day research seminar aimed at equipping master’s students with practical approaches and techniques for conducting graduate-level research.

Held under the theme “Approaches and Techniques of Doing Research at Master's Level,” the seminar saw a collection of postgraduate students at Master's level converge in Mpanga Library for an intensive and interactive engagement with facilitators who guided participants through key aspects of academic research, from proposal development to scholarly publication.

The seminar started yesterday, 16th May 2026, with a series of presentations focused on Research Introduction, Literature Review, research design, data analysis, and academic publication, areas the university says are critical in preparing graduate students for independent inquiry and evidence-based scholarly research.

Prof. Faustino Orach Meza and Dr. Anne Abaho took charge of day one, while Dr. Fred Lwanga, Prof. Wilson Muyinda Mande, and Prof. Solomon Muchwa Asiimwe handled day 2.

Dr. Fred Lwanga led sessions on research methodology, taking students through research design, study population, sampling techniques, data collection methods and tools, as well as data processing and analysis. He emphasized the importance of aligning research methods with study objectives to ensure the validity and reliability of findings. “Research begins with clarity of purpose. Once students understand the problem they are investigating, the methodology becomes the bridge that leads them to credible findings,” Dr Lwanga told participants.

Prof. Wilson Muyinda Mande delivered a session on the format for presentation and analysis of research findings, taking students through the university’s academic guidelines for structuring dissertations. His presentation also covered interpretation of findings, drawing conclusions, and making recommendations based on evidence generated through fieldwork.

He urged students to pay close attention to how research findings are presented, noting that quality academic work is measured not only by data collection but by how clearly evidence is analyzed and communicated. “Good research should tell a story through evidence. The presentation of findings determines whether your work can influence policy, scholarship, or practice,” Prof Mande said.

The seminar also addressed scholarly publishing, with Prof. Solomon M. Asiimwe taking participants through the process of converting dissertations into publishable journal articles or books. He explained the authorship process, noting that a student normally takes first authorship while the supervisor appears as second author, depending on contribution.

Prof. Asiimwe said successful publication requires a well-developed manuscript, adherence to academic standards, subject mastery, and proper structuring of scholarly papers. "Completing a dissertation should not mark the end of research. Students should aim to transform their findings into articles and books that contribute to knowledge and society,” he said.

Tweet_Text:
Nkumba University has kicked off a two-day research seminar aimed at strengthening the research capacity of master’s students, as the institution intensifies efforts to improve postgraduate studies.

Organized under the Directorate of Postgraduate Studies and Research, the seminar started today, 16th May, and will run through Sunday, 17th, at the university’s main Library, Mpanga.

In his opening remarks, the Director of Postgraduate Studies and Research, Associate Prof. Solomon Asiimwe Muchwa, said sound methodology remains the foundation of any credible academic inquiry, urging students to view research not only as a graduation requirement but as a contribution to solving societal challenges. "Research is the backbone of any meaningful scholarly work. A good research topic alone is not enough; what gives credibility, reliability, and academic value to research is the methodology employed,” Prof Muchwa said.

While dissecting Chapter One of the research proposal development, Prof. Faustino Orach-Meza said students must pay close attention to the theoretical foundations of their studies.

He emphasized that a strong proposal should clearly articulate the background to the study, statement of the problem, purpose, objectives, research questions, scope, and significance, while identifying the gap in existing knowledge. “The theoretical background provides the established principles on which the study is anchored. It helps researchers understand what has already been studied and what remains unanswered,” he said.

Dr Anne Abaho, who handled a session on literature review, challenged students to critically engage with existing studies instead of doing the obvious by summarizing published work. “We are reading in between the lines. As you review literature, you must identify weaknesses and unanswered questions. That is where your research gap lies,” she said.

She advises that students should focus on peer-reviewed sources and credible academic databases, rather than relying on unverified online content. "Do not be carried away by everything you find online. Assess the relevance, authority, and accuracy of every source. The quality of your literature review determines the strength of your study,” she added.

Vice Chancellor Prof. Jude Lubega cautioned against plagiarism and reminded students to strictly follow the university’s postgraduate research guidelines to ensure quality and consistency in their dissertations.

University officials say such seminars are intended to provide clarifications on the dos and don'ts in scholarly research, especially for students beginning dissertation work.

The seminar continues tomorrow with sessions focusing on research methodology, presentation and analysis of research findings, and how to publish a journal article or book from a dissertation.

Tweet_Text: Le diplôme de doctorat est le plus élevé des grades universitaires. Il vous donne le grade et titre de DOCTEUR (Dr) dans votre discipline scientifique. Il existe une véritable obsession à être appelé DOCTEUR ici au Kenya. Surtout chez les intellectuels issus d’une région particulière. Chez l’élite LUO, être PhD relève beaucoup plus du rang social dans la communauté que d’un titre académique. J’ai toujours eu ma propre compréhension au sujet du diplôme de doctorat. En trois points: 1] Le doctorat est utile si vous voulez travailler dans la recherche et l’enseignement. Si vous voulez etre un juriste (avocat ou juge), un Master en Droit+stage suffit. Mais si vous voulez enseigner à la FAC de droit, poursuivez votre doctorat ; 2]. Le diplôme de doctorat ne vous  donne pas accès directement à un emploi. Dans le monde professionnel, le recruteur choisira  un candidat avec un diplôme de Licence + 3 ans d’expérience professionnelle à un docteur sans expérience professionnelle; 3] Mon conseil aux jeunes diplômés: si vous venez d’obtenir votre diplôme de Licence, je vous conseille de chercher un emploi payé ou pas, ou bien un stage professionnel avant de penser à poursuivre vos études au niveau doctorat. L’expérience professionnelle est plus importante qu’un diplôme de doctorat.

As part of its nationwide awareness drive in partnership with the Teachers Service Commission @TSC_KE, the Public Service Superannuation Fund (PSSF) is currently holding member engagement sessions with Teachers Training Colleges across the country. These sessions are running alongside the ongoing retooling programme for senior school teachers.

The sensitisation seeks to deepen teachers’ understanding of the contributory pension scheme. Teachers, who form more than 60% of the Fund’s total membership, are being urged to actively interact with PSSF’s digital platforms — particularly the member portal at https://t.co/oy3t0v9gbW — where they can track their contribution statements, view interest earned, and access other self-service tools.

A key message during the engagements has been the importance of updating beneficiary nomination records to ensure that benefits are passed on swiftly and without dispute in the event of a member’s death.

The Public Service Superannuation Fund (PSSF) is deepening its collaboration with the Teachers Service Commission (@TSC_KE) to boost retirement benefits for teachers.

In a courtesy visit, PSSF CEO @DrAiyabeiJonah met with Acting TSC CEO Eveleen Mitei to explore new avenues for partnership. The TSC represents 321,961 of PSSF’s members, making it a critical stakeholder in the scheme’s operations.

Ms Mitei praised PSSF’s Board of Trustees for its strong performance since the scheme’s operationalisation four years ago, calling it a “significant win” for advancing teachers’ retirement security. She reaffirmed TSC’s commitment to timely contribution remittances to maximize returns for members.

“Continuous member education is essential to raise awareness among teachers,” Ms Mitei said, noting plans to integrate PSSF member education programs into induction programs for new teachers. The partnership will also tap TSC’s communication channels to broaden outreach.

Dr Aiyabei formally invited TSC CEO and commissioners to the upcoming PSSF Pan-African Pensions Conference, where Ms Mitei will join a panel discussion with other employers on retirement reforms. Participants can register to attend the conference through https://t.co/L7T9uZw6jI

Tweet_Text:
Transforming Lives Through Skills Training

The Presidential Initiative on Skilling the Girl/Boy Child was launched to address the high unemployment rates among youth, particularly those who lack access to formal education. At Luzira Prisons, young men and women are trained in various vocational disciplines, including tailoring, knitting, confectionery, bakery, hairdressing, and weaving These skills not only provide a means of livelihood but also reduce vulnerability to crime, poverty, and social marginalization.

Many of the beneficiaries, some of whom come from underprivileged backgrounds, have successfully used their acquired skills to start businesses, gain employment, and uplift their communities. The program has also played a key role in reducing youth delinquency by offering a constructive alternative to those who might have been at risk of engaging in illegal activities.

Meet our New Deputy Minister for Education under the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports.
Hon. Mutegeki Nathan Atwoki is a dedicated, resourceful, and goal-driven professional educator (teacher) with a strong commitment to  social and academic growth and development of every child. He possesses an exceptional ability to establish professional relationships with parents, academic staff, and administration.

Hon. Mutegeki Nathan Atwoki's educational background includes a Post Graduate Diploma in Educational Management Planning and Administration from @NkumbaUni, a Bachelor's of Education (Primary) from Ibanda University, a Diploma in Education Primary from Kampala International University, a Grade III Teachers Certificate from Canon Apolo Core PTC, a UCE Certificate from Kahinju Secondary School (Fort Portal), and primary level education at Busita Primary School in Bunyangabu.

He is currently the Headteacher of Bugando Primary School in Ntoroko District Local Government and Previously served as Headteacher of Rwamabale Primary School, Deputy Headteacher of Kamuhigi Primary School, and as a classroom teacher at St. Charles Lwanga Primary School in Fort Portal City.

Beyond his professional roles in education, Hon. Mutegeki Nathan Atwoki is also a spokesperson for all Tooro cultural functions, a role he was trained for by Kogere Foundation Uganda. Additionally, he is the Chairperson of the Barungu Development Initiative.

Meet Owekitinisa Mutegeki Ronald Araali (@araalimutegeki),  our New Deputy Information Minister. Ronald is a resident of Bunyangabu County and brings a wealth of experience in education, culture and media.  

He holds a Master’s degree in Education and Policy Studies and a Bachelor’s degree in Education. Currently, he is a visiting lecturer at Uganda Pentecostal University (UPU) and teaches at Kiyombya Seed Secondary School and King of Kings College, Fort Portal.  

Owekitinisa Mutegeki Ronald has been an active member of the Kingdom Supreme Council (Orukurato Orukuru orw'obukama), serving as a Special Nominee of His Majesty the King. 

He also has a strong background in media, having worked as a radio presenter and moderator at Dana FM.

Additionally, he has been a frequent panelist on various radio and television talk shows, contributing to public discourse on key issues.  

Araali is a Mucwezi, Mwihwa w’Abaranzi, & Mwijukuru w’Ababiito n’Abagorongwa. His appointment will strengthen the Kingdom’s communications department, ensuring effective dissemination of information and engagement with the people of Toro.
#TooroKingdomVision2045

Dr. Alice Kateregga, a Neurosurgeon Resident, delivered a thought-provoking presentation on "Surgical Errors: A Surgical Resident Cohort Experience." She shared valuable insights and recommendations to prevent surgical errors:

Key Takeaways:

1. Education and Policy: Implementing comprehensive training programs and clear policies to ensure surgeons are equipped with the knowledge and protocols necessary to minimize errors.
2. Communication Systems and Checkpoints: Establishing robust communication channels and checkpoints throughout the surgical process to catch potential errors before they occur.
3. Patient Advocacy: Encouraging active patient involvement in their care, ensuring their concerns are heard and addressed, and promoting a culture of transparency.
4. Advocacy for Surgeons: Supporting surgeons in their professional development, providing resources for continuous learning, and fostering a culture of psychological safety to encourage open discussion of errors..
Dr. Alice Kateregga's presentation highlighted the importance of a multifaceted approach to preventing surgical errors, emphasizing the need for education, effective communication, patient-centered care, and support for surgeons. #ASOUConference2025

Mr. Amos Turyashaba, Commissioner of Prisons (SACCO Chairperson), delivered his report at the 17th Prisons SACCO AGM, stressing the institution's significant strides and challenges.

He began by outlining the SACCO's achievements over the past year, including:

- Member Education and Financial Literacy: Successfully carried out member education and financial literacy sensitization in 8 regions.
- Membership Growth: Enrolled 252 new members, increasing the membership base by 2% from 12,880 to 13,132.
- Digital Platform: Successfully launched and rolled out the MSACCO digital platform, with 49% of active members already on board.
- Loan Disbursement: Recorded 11.4% growth in loan disbursement.
- Asset Base: Improved the asset base by 10% from 10.41BN to 11.45BN.
- Equity Capital Ratio: Maintained a higher equity capital ratio of 70.6%.

SECONDARY EDUCATION
 
Jinja District:
 
Jinja District has 10 Government secondary schools and 63 private secondary schools. Eight (8) Government secondary schools Provide Universal Secondary Education and Universal Post Ordinary Level Education and Training with a total enrollment of 8,746.
 
Jinja City:
 
Jinja City has 10 Government secondary schools and 79 private secondary schools. Only Four (4) Government secondary schools Provide Universal Secondary Education and Universal Post Ordinary Level Education and Training with a total enrollment of 4,990.
 
DISTRIBUTION OF GOVERNMENT SECONDARY SCHOOLS IN JINJA DISTRICT AND JINJA CITY
 
Jinja District:
 
Jinja District has 07 Sub-Counties, with 06 hosting at least one Government secondary school, while 1 Sub-County is still lacking.

Jinja City:
 
It has 2 Sub-Counties with all the 2 hosting at least one Government secondary school.
 
CONSTRUCTION OF SEED SCHOOLS

Tweet_Text:
Must read by all Africans.

Dictatorland: The Men Who Stole Africa by Paul Kenyon

Kenyon gives potted biographies consisting of short, punchy sections, scenes depicting the origins, education and early years of each baddie, their early involvement with their country’s independence movements or army (training ground for most dictators) or with a nationalist guerrilla movement.

The dictator who grew so rich on his country's cocoa crop that he built a 35-storey-high basilica in the jungles of the Ivory Coast. The austere, incorruptible leader who has shut Eritrea off from the world in a permanent state of war and conscripted every adult into the armed forces. 

In Equatorial Guinea, the paranoid despot who thought Hitler was the saviour of Africa and waged a relentless campaign of terror against his own people. 

And behind these almost incredible stories of fantastic violence and excess lie the dark secrets of Western greed and complicity, the insatiable taste for chocolate, oil, diamonds and gold that has encouraged dictators to rule with an iron hand, siphoning off their share of the action into mansions in Paris and banks in Zurich and keeping their people in dire poverty.

Tweet_Text:
Under Kibaki, life had a rhythm people could feel. Food on the table meant something. Schools were within reach. Hospitals did not feel like waiting rooms for death. Under Uhuru, despite the noise, there was still a functioning system holding education, health, agriculture and security together in a fragile but visible balance.

Today, Kenyans are not arguing about promises anymore. They are arguing about survival. Hunger is not a debate, it is a daily experience. Health care is collapsing under the weight of empty pockets. Education is slipping back into struggle. Farmers are squeezed, families are stretched, and dignity is being eroded in real time.

Yet in the middle of this pain, what we are shown are roads and ceremonies, ribbons and speeches, as if asphalt can replace food or projects can silence empty stomachs.

This is not governance that uplifts a nation. It is governance that celebrates appearances while citizens carry the burden of reality.

Kenyans are not asking for miracles. They are asking not to suffer in their own country. Ruto must Go!

Heartfelt congratulations to Nkalubo Dauda, the Dean of Students at Metropolitan International University, upon earning your Master’s Degree in Public Administration from Metropolitan International University during its 8th Graduation Ceremony held at the Mbarara Campus.

This is not just a personal academic milestone; it is a victory for leadership, service, and vision. As Dean of Students, you have consistently demonstrated commitment to student welfare, discipline, mentorship, and institutional growth. Advancing your education in Public Administration is a powerful reflection of your dedication to effective governance, strategic management, and transformative leadership.

A Master’s Degree in Public Administration is more than a qualification — it is a statement of readiness to serve at a higher level, to influence policy, to strengthen administrative systems, and to inspire excellence within and beyond the university community. Your achievement sends a clear message to students: that learning never stops, that leaders must grow, and that academic excellence and service can walk hand in hand.

Your journey will undoubtedly inspire many young scholars at Metropolitan International University to aim higher, work harder, and believe in the power of education. As you celebrate this remarkable accomplishment, may it open even greater doors of impact, innovation, and national service.

Congratulations once again on this well-deserved achievement. The entire university community is proud of you.

Chuka University, Chuka Town, Tharaka Nithi County 
Thursday, February 19, 2026

Electronic Government Procurement (e-GP) Syatem Phase One Capacity Building - Chuka University. 

The Secretary of Administration (SA) in the State Department for Public Investments and Assets Management Dr. Bahati Mwita today represented the Principal Secretary Mr. Cyrell Wagunda in the ongoing phase one training of suppliers on electronic Government Procurement (eGP) System today morning at Chuka University, in Chuka Town. 

The capacity building exercise happening across 20 counties concurrently is a joint venture between the State Department and the Kenya National Chamber of Commerce and Industry (KNCCI) which is aims at creating awareness and taking through the suppliers and contractors the new mode procurement rolled out at the beginning of the current Fiscal Year.

In her remarks, Dr. Mwita affirmed that the system is not only poised to bring efficiency in service delivery but also seal corruption loopholes by promoting transparency.

"Once you upload your documents, they become permanent records which no one can manipulate. This is a significant departure from incidences where suppliers documents were being expunged by unprofessional procurement officials so they can engage their preferred suppliers," said the SA.

She lauded the newfound partnership between the State Department and KNCCI saying it is one way of enhancing digital superhighway in line with the Bottom-up Economic Transformation Agenda (BETA).

"The Government is making significant steps in reducing paperwork and making most of its services go digital. At the comfort of your home, you can monitor the tenders published and track the process yourself," she added.

Mr. Wilfred Siakama, the eGP team leader and Trainer of Trainees (ToT) said over 1,500 procuring entities have been published and over 35,000 suppliers have been trained and approved to do business with the government. 

Phase one of the capacity building fora ends tomorrow ahead of the roll out of phase two next week.

#ServiceDelivery
#Inclusivity
#LeavingNoOneBehind
#SDPIAMDelivers

Martin Ssentiba Wins Nkumba University Guild Presidential Race 

International Relations and Diplomacy student, Martin Ssentiba Ssempijja has been declared the winner of the Nkumba University Guild Presidential race, edging his closest rival in a tightly contested election.

Ssentiba scooped 465 votes, defeating National Resistance Movement (NRM) flag bearer Makumai Deograciou Anyama, who garnered 431 votes. Koy Clinton Nasasira trailed with 241 votes. The results were announced by the Guild Electoral Commission Chairperson, Tumukuratire Eryabu, following what he described as a “peaceful but highly competitive” poll.

The margin of just 34 votes between Ssentiba and Anyama defined a race that remained too close to call until the final tally, with both camps maintaining strong grassroots mobilization efforts across campus.

Speaking shortly after his victory, Ssentiba struck a conciliatory tone, emphasizing unity and collective responsibility. "Thank you, thank you, thank you… This victory belongs to all of us, the students of Nkumba University,” he said. “We have had sleepless nights reaching out to every corner of this campus. Now the real task begins, to deliver on our promises, and I will need all hands on deck.”

Ssentiba’s win had been anticipated in part by pre-election opinion polls conducted by Nkumba University News, which placed him in a commanding lead. Analysts attributed his early momentum to his prompt declaration of candidacy and a campaign message anchored on student welfare, inclusivity, and strengthening the university’s external visibility.

His manifesto, which resonated with a broad section of the student body, prioritized improved internet connectivity, stronger engagement with university management, and a more inclusive campus environment. 

During a recent televised debate on Kingdom TV, Ssentiba positioned himself as a pragmatic negotiator committed to bridging the gap between students and administration. "I am prepared to engage authorities constructively to ensure student welfare is not just discussed but acted upon,” he said while appearing on KTV, highlighting the urgency of reliable internet access as a key academic enabler.

The election, however, was not without its dynamics. Political observers noted the midweek arrival of NRM strategists on campus as a potential game-changer in favor of Anyama. Yet, the three-way race, particularly the presence of Nasasira, appears to have split the vote in a manner that ultimately worked to Ssentiba’s advantage.

Despite the high stakes, voter turnout was lower than expected, with approximately 1,200 students casting ballots. The Students’ Electoral Commission had earlier urged compliance with voting requirements, including tuition clearance and full enrolment on the student portal, conditions that some students cited as barriers to participation.

In recent years, the guild presidency has carried heightened expectations, with students increasingly demanding accountability, transparency, and tangible improvements in welfare.

Ssentiba now assumes office at a time when student leadership is under scrutiny, following mixed reactions to the outgoing administration of Mawanda Derrick, which some students criticized as being overly rigid in its approach to governance.

At 23, Ssentiba brings a track record of leadership, having previously served in various capacities including head prefect and student leader within campus. His supporters describe him as articulate and strategic, qualities they believe will be essential in navigating the complexities of university governance.

Good news to kick off the weekend!
We are delighted to celebrate the successful doctoral thesis defenses of our staff members Dr. Brigitte Mukarunyana and Dr. Albert Ndagijimana, who earned their PhDs from Umeå University through the @URSwedenProgram

Dr. Mukarunyana’s research presents an innovative and sustainable approach to water treatment by transforming agricultural waste into effective materials for water purification.

Dr. Ndagijimana’s study on childhood stunting in Rwanda’s Northern Province sheds light on the underlying drivers of malnutrition and provides evidence to support localized, family-centered interventions that can improve child nutrition outcomes.

Congratulations to both researchers on this remarkable achievement. We appreciate their impactful contributions and look forward to seeing their findings translated into solutions that benefit communities in #Rwanda and beyond!

We have officially entered into a partnership with Somali National University to strengthen academic and research collaboration, with a particular focus on genocide studies, peace and conflict, governance, and politics.

The agreement was signed today by DVC  Dr. Raymond Ndikumana and Deputy Rector Dr. Fuad Mire Hassan of Somali National University, with Richard Niwenshuti, CEO of @Cooperation_RW witnessing the ceremony.

The partnership will promote joint research, curriculum co-development, academic program development, and the harmonization of teaching and training materials. CRIME🚨: Rugby Star Gongodyo Killed in Suspected Mob Attack in Upper Naguru.

Police in Kampala are investigating a case of mob justice that left a university student dead in Upper Naguru Community Village, Bukoto I Parish, Nakawa Division. 

The incident occurred on 5 June 2026 at around 2:00pm and has been registered at Kira Road Police Station as murder by mob action.

The deceased has been identified as 27-year-old Gongodyo Sydney, a student of Makerere University and resident of Najjera in Kira Municipality, Wakiso District. According to police, Gongodyo was attacked and subjected to mob action by members of the public under still unclear circumstances. Officers responded to the scene and rushed him to Mulago National Referral Hospital for treatment, but he succumbed to his injuries at about 7:00pm the same day.

Kampala Metropolitan Police spokesperson SP Kawala Racheal said investigations are underway to establish the circumstances leading to the attack and to identify and arrest all those involved, adding that further details will be shared as inquiries progress.

KAMPENI YA MJUE JIRANI YAKO, NAMANGA ONE STOP BORDER

Idara ya Uhamiaji Mkoa wa Arusha, leo tarehe 17/02/2025, imepokea wanafunzi wa Shule ya Sekondari St. Jude, Kidato cha Tano, katika kituo cha Namanga One Stop Border Post, ambapo walijifunza masuala ya Kiuhamiaji mpakani.

Wanafunzi hao walielezwa kuhusu:
✅ Usimamizi wa mipaka – uingiaji na utokaji wa wageni nchini.
✅ Huduma za viza kwa wageni waingiao Tanzania.
✅ Doria na misako kudhibiti wahamiaji haramu.
✅ Teknolojia ya Uhamiaji Mtandao kwa utoaji wa huduma bora.
✅ Kampeni ya "Mjue Jirani Yako" – kukuza uelewano na mshikamano kati ya wahamiaji na wakazi wa asili.

Utoaji wa elimu hii ni zoezi endelevu ili kuhakikisha wananchi wanapata uelewa wa masuala mbalimbali ya Kiuhamiaji na umuhimu wa kushiriki katika kampeni ya "Mjue Jirani Yako" kwa usalama wa jamii na taifa.

WANAFUNZI MKOANI DODOMA WASHAURIWA KUJIUNGA NA KLABU ZA UHAMIAJI

Na. A/Insp Rose Mligo - Dodoma

Idara ya Uhamiaji Mkoa wa Dodoma, leo tarehe 17 Februari 2025, imetoa elimu ya masuala ya Kiuhamiaji katika Shule ya Sekondari Kikuyu, huku ikiwasihi wanafunzi kujiunga na Klabu za Uhamiaji zitakazoanzishwa shuleni hapo. Klabu hizi zitawasaidia wanafunzi kuelewa wajibu wao katika ulinzi na usalama wa nchi, kama inavyoelekezwa katika Ibara ya Pili ya Katiba ya Jamhuri ya Muungano wa Tanzania.

✅ Mkaguzi Msaidizi wa Uhamiaji Vicent Missana alitoa elimu kuhusu Uraia wa Tanzania, akifafanua sheria na kanuni za uombaji wa uraia.
✅ Mkaguzi Msaidizi wa Uhamiaji Joseph Mgoba alielezea Vibali, Pasi, na taratibu za uombaji wa hati za safari kwa wanafunzi na watumishi.
✅ Konstebo Joyce Malima aliwasihi wanafunzi kujiunga na Klabu ya Uhamiaji, akisisitiza faida zake hasa katika kupata elimu ya kampeni ya "Mjue Jirani Yako" na namna ya kushiriki katika ulinzi wa jamii yao kwa kutoa taarifa kuhusu wahamiaji haramu.

Wanafunzi wamehimizwa kushiriki kikamilifu katika Klabu za Uhamiaji ili kupata maarifa sahihi kuhusu masuala ya Kiuhamiaji, Uraia, na Usalama wa Taifa.

#UhamiajiUsalamaNaMaendeleo🇹🇿 #MjueJiraniYako🇹🇿

UHAMIAJI DODOMA YATOA ELIMU KATIKA SHULE YA SEKONDARI KISASA

Na. Mwandishi Wetu - Dodoma

Ofisi ya Uhamiaji Mkoa wa Dodoma, leo tarehe 18 Februari 2025, imetoa elimu ya masuala ya Uhamiaji katika Shule ya Sekondari Kisasa, ikiwa ni moja ya shule zilizoanzisha Klabu ya Uhamiaji kwa lengo la kupandikiza mbegu ya uzalendo miongoni mwa wanafunzi na kuwaelimisha kuhusu udhibiti wa wahamiaji haramu.

Naibu Afisa Uhamiaji Mkoa wa Dodoma, Mrakibu wa Uhamiaji Namdula P. Komba, aliwaeleza wanafunzi umuhimu wa elimu ya Uhamiaji kwa jamii, kazi za Idara ya Uhamiaji, vibali vya ukaazi, pasi, viza, pamoja na wahamiaji haramu na madhara yao kwa nchi. Pia, aliwasihi wanafunzi kuwa huru kuuliza maswali yanayohusiana na elimu wanayopewa na Maafisa Uhamiaji wanaofika shuleni hapo.

Kwa upande wake, Mkaguzi Msaidizi wa Uhamiaji Excel Nziku alitoa ufafanuzi kuhusu utaratibu wa utoaji wa pasipoti, aina za pasipoti, pamoja na mada ya uraia.

Elimu hii imepokelewa vizuri na wanafunzi na walimu, huku Klabu ya Uhamiaji shuleni hapo ikipata idadi kubwa ya wanafunzi kuliko ilivyotarajiwa, ikionyesha mwitikio chanya wa wanafunzi katika kushiriki masuala ya Uhamiaji na uzalendo.
